I gave it some more thought. The insight was right, but I am not convinced anymore that it implies we must abolish the `add_file` resolution. Here's why:

In all cases except PAGE-XML files, we can have multiple files per page/grp/type. So there we'd always lay a trap: if we intervene, we might effectively create an artificial clash (e.g. by disallowing multiple textline images); if we don't, we might miss an actual clash (e.g. by allowing two competing ALTO-XML outputs).

So page/grp/type clashes cannot be decided.

Then how about ID clashes? In the benign case, the same kind of process that is responsible for the existing ID is now repeating. So we should be fine just checking `force/overwrite` mode (hence, reuse vs. raise). In the malignant case, some other process created the same ID, despite being unrelated by the matter (i.e. different page/grp/type). Suppose that we _can_ distinguish that case, how do we proceed? 

We cannot control the process' `make_file_id` (as we are just in a subsequent/subordinate call), and we cannot rename the existing ID either (as it might cause inconsistencies elsewhere). So I'd say we are forced to capitulate (i.e. raise).

That would amount to a much simplified decision tree. Am I missing something?

Hm, I tend to agree that returning the response is not the best idea, though it has been in the adapter since it was introduced: https://github.com/hvac/hvac/pull/537/files#diff-db919c05d0fa65b24b7b140bab3fd3530b3530735e4e9e20a843881557158936

There is a `RawAdapter` that can be used when the full response object is desired, and that's the superclass for this already. 

I suppose the way we can look at this is that this is, specifically, the `JSONAdapter`, so a response that's not valid or parseable as JSON could (should?) be an exception. 

I don't think it should be an empty dictionary in this case, because it implies that everything went ok but really there was a response there that's now hidden.

The main thing we need to be careful of is that this is the default adapter, but then again, if anyone was using this full response object I'd guess it was mostly to deal with 204s, since 4xx and 5xx codes would already be raising exceptions.

> Why nest enabled under pyproject? I don't think we do that anywhere else. Are we expecting to add more options under pyproject in the future? Typically, we'd just have a single top-level key like generatePyProject or something like that, and if we did have additional related flags we wanted in the future, we'd just add those as their own top-level keys.

I expect many of the fields are overridable. _e.g._: `classifiers`, `authors`, `maintainers`. 
There are some fields that we can reuse from our provider schema without having to override, but maybe we want to allow the override anyway. For example, if someone wants the `keywords` given to PyPi to differ from the `keywords` used in the Pulumi registry. These fields are `description`, `license`, `keywords`, and `readme`.

If we can see a world where any of these fields are overrideable, we should probably make `pyproject` an Object, and allow those overrides to be specified within the Object. It's an attempt to future-proof against having to add more top-level fields. I suspect using a boolean `generatePyProject` field might not be enough to cover the universe of configurables for this feature.

Another approach is to remove `enabled`, and just use the presence or absence of the `pyproject` key to indicate whether the feature is toggled.

When `self` is `null`, we don't pass along a `__self__` arg to the `Call` RPC and don't use it to determine the provider (if you want to explicitly specify the provider, you'd have to do it via `CallOptions`).

This is groundwork to enable module-level functions that use `Call` instead of `Invoke`, to allow module-level functions to accept `Input<T>`. We've deferred this for now, but eventually we want to have a way in the schema to opt-in to this for module-level functions so they can accept `Input<T>`, and these will use `Call` without a `__self__` arg, rather than `Invoke`.

The reason for the new `Call` RPC (rather than reusing `Invoke`) is to make it easier for provider authors to implement `Call` -- we provide helpers that adapt the raw RPC values into the Pulumi SDK model of each language (e.g. the values are converted back into `Output<T>` as needed) in the provider.

Regarding your first point `u.Unit("cm*1")`: it's a nice trick, but looks like a Hack! What about replacing "1" with the empty string "" ?  It seems that that's how Astropy writes u.dimensionless_unscaled units anyway: 
```
u.Unit("1").__str__()
>> ""
u.Unit("").__str__()
>> ""
u.Unit("") == u.dimensionless_unscaled
>> True
```
And no need for the Hack then! 

---

Second point:

> the most commonly brought up argument is that peak/max normalization leads to erroneous results

It's not erroneous for emission emission spectra (if you account for the change in units), and sometimes useful: if what you're interested is the maximum energy Flux, for instance in radiative transfer, you'd get a result directly in energy flux  "~W/cm2/sr", instead of spectral energy flux "~W/cm2/sr/nm" ). 
For absorption spectrum, it's erroenous as I described it in my previous comment: because the underlying quantity is a ratio, so we assume that the units of the background spectrum did not change. 

What you're describing (given intensities separately) is possible. Actually, users could do :
```
s1 = Radiance(emission_spectrum)
s2 = Radiance(background_spectrum)
transmittance = s1/s2               # I'm not sure if this work for Spectrum objects, but same is possible using s1.get(), s2.get() ? 
```
But usually your instrument, or the instrument software, will process this directly and return a transmittance spectrum directly. So the main use case takes "I/I0". 

---

>  Can't we normalize the spectrum after passing it through the slit?

We could but that's not what the user asked for, and it would become very dangerous! We better raise an error saying that applying a non-area-normalized slit to a transmittance spectrum is erroneous, and that he should do otherwise. 

We could suggest a solution in the error message: 

```
# during error handling: 
if 'radiance_noslit' in self.get_vars():
    raise ValueError("ValueError("""applying a non-area-normalized slit to a transmittance spectrum is erroneous. Use `method="area"` in apply_slit(). If you're only interested in the emission spectrum, you can extract the emission spectrum the first: `s_rad = Radiance_noslit(s).apply_slit(...)`""")
else:
    raise ValueError("ValueError("""applying a non-area-normalized slit to a transmittance spectrum is erroneous. Use `method="area"` in apply_slit()""")

```

---

Hope this is clear! 
Also whatever physics class you took does not really matter here: it's more "technicalities" (for instance, knowledge of instruments & instrument software) than physics and science at this point! :)

From https://docs.python.org/3.4/reference/datamodel.html#object.__repr__
> If at all possible, this should look like a valid Python expression that could be used to recreate an object with the same value (given an appropriate environment).

Usually this means You'll want the `__module__` and `__qualname__` of the class in there along with the args.

E.g.
```py
def __repr__(self):
   return "{}.{}({})".format(self.__class__.__module__, self.__class__.__qualname__, ...)

While I agree having such scripts is very helpful in transitioning projects from older config definitions to new model, I am not sure if it makes sense to include this in OpenThread GitHub master branch. OT master does not support any of the older config definitions. I think including such a script can be confusing to a new developer/user of OT exploring the code.

In the history of development of OpenThread, we have had changes to the public OT APIs. Two larger ones that I recall:

- [PR #1764](https://github.com/openthread/openthread/pull/1764) where `otError` enumeration names  were changed from `kThreadError<ErrorInCamelCase>` model to `OT_ERROR_<ErrorInUpperCase>` model (the type itself was renamed `ThreadError` -> `otError`).
- [PR #1173 ](https://github.com/openthread/openthread/pull/1173) where many of public APIs and definitions were renamed to follow a consistent coding/naming convention. 

In such cases, we used scripts and tools to update all internal projects and convert the old naming model to new one, but we did not merge/include the scripts in the OpenThread master (github master branch). 

Thoughts?

Yeah sorry this is a little misleading. I'm not clearing anything from the functionapp definition (what we use in the request to make the function app).

These two variables (`site_config_json` and `app_settings_json`) only contain json from the stacks API, and represent what app settings and site configs we'll be setting  on the functionapp definition _later_ -- [here specifically](https://github.com/Azure/azure-cli/blob/dev/src/azure-cli/azure/cli/command_modules/appservice/custom.py#L2826-L2833). Since linux custom image apps shouldn't be using the stacks api (they're providing their own image that contains these app settings instead of using our image and stacks api settings), we're just pre-emptively clearing these settings before they overwrite what's in the functionapp definition.

In a nutshell we're saying -- "Since you're providing your own image and settings, we'll go ahead and clear the settings we would normally set before we set them, especially since we don't even know what runtime your custom image is using."

According to Cisco doc, the right flag is ```section```: https://www.cisco.com/c/en/us/td/docs/ios/fundamentals/command/reference/cf_book/cf_s1.html

```
Examples

The following examples compare the filtering characteristics of the show running-config | include command with the show running-config | section command. The first example gathers just the lines from the configuration file with "interface" in them.

Router# show running-config | include interface


interface Ethernet0/0 
interface Ethernet1/0 
interface Serial2/0 
interface Serial3/0


The next example uses the show command section command to gather the lines in the configuration file with "interface" in them as well as any lines associated with those entries. In this example, interface configuration information is captured.

Router# show running-config | section include interface


interface Ethernet0/0 
 shutdown 
 no cdp enable

interface Ethernet1/0 
 shutdown 
 no cdp enable 
interface Serial2/0 
 shutdown 
 no cdp enable 
interface Serial3/0 
 shutdown 
 no cdp enable
```

@YannickJadoul -- you are correct to point out that switching to `MinSizeRel` at a global level indeed gives size-based optimizations. The point of this PR is to do so selectively on a per-target level. A very common project layout is:

1. `libmy_project.so` -- all the expensive computation happens in here, optimize as much as possible with auto-vectorization, `-march=native -O3`, etc.
2. `my_project_cli`: linking to `lib_myproject.so` to expose functionality via command line interface
3. `my_project.cpython-..so`: Python bindings. These can become huge (~10-100MB) if the bindings are very comprehensive. Yet, performance here isn't all that critical, because we're just forwarding a bunch of arguments to long-running algorithms that live in the other shared library.

So the goal of this PR is to compile parts (1+2) in release mode, while using MinSizeRel just for the binding target (3).

When specifying a sequence of optimization flags, the latter one have precedence, at least for our compilers in the "officially supported" list.

Yes, both `kernel` and `ellipsoid_intensity` contain redundant information, and I was trying to remove the multiplication in favor of not computing the positions. Turns out that that is a bad idea though, because the compiler stops vectorizing the loop. I suspect this is because it can't guarantee that consecutive elements of the loop access contiguous elements in memory anymore and thus doesn't load in a vectorized fashion.

Does SSE/AVX have instructions for vectorized comparison? I will need to check that. If not, I think multiplication will turn out faster.

> correct me if I'm wrong

You got the loops correct; the second loop of the kernel is over the last dimension of it to exploit vectorized loading/processing on contiguous data. It's the same outer/inner split as `np.nditer(flags=["external_loop"])`.

You did get the application of the kernel backwards though. I'm not pushing down the ellipsoid as I iterate the kernel; it can certainly be done in this way though. Instead, I do it the other way around. For each position in the kernel I assume that the ball is fully submerged when it touches that position and I pre-compute how much higher the center is than that position. This is stored in `ellipsoid_intensity`. I can add this difference to the actual pixel value at that position - this gives me the background intensity if the ball actually touches there - and then find the global minimum over the kernel. This works, because the ellipsoid surface is strictly concave and has it's maximum underneath the pixel being estimated.

The context would setup the handler and return the `StringIO`. When exiting it could do any clean up that it needs (remove the handler so it won't affect other tests down the line?). 

An easy way make a context manager is with [`contextlib`](https://docs.python.org/3.8/library/contextlib.html):

```python
from contextlib import contextmanager

@contextmanager
def capture_log():        
    log_file = io.StringIO()
    handler = logging.StreamHandler(log_file)
    get_logger().addHandler(handler)
    # return this to the context and halt until exit
    yield log_file
    # executed on exit
    get_logger().removeHandler(handler)
```

I deleted test case `test_s3_delete_non_existent_object`. It can pass CI, but I realize it can only be reproduced by `moto.mock_s3`.

That is, when we use `moto.mock_s3` to mirror the S3 environment, deleting an non-existent object would result in an `Errors` entry in the response. But in AWS S3 and IBM COS (S3-compatible), deleting non-existent object is allowed, and the non-existent key would still be in the `Deleted` entry.

**Regarding `S3DeleteObjectsOperator()`, if there is `Errors` entry in the response, the operator will raise exception and fail.**

**Reference**:
https://boto3.amazonaws.com/v1/documentation/api/latest/reference/services/s3.html#S3.Client.delete_objects

    'RequestCharged': 'requester',
    'Errors': [
        {
            'Key': 'string',

}

> Are you aware of any Predictor usage examples in a third-party packages?

I'm not aware of any such usage. It is *technically* possible for user code to get a `Predictor` using the public API of the R package.

You'd have to use `lgb.train()` or `lightgbm()` to get a `Booster`, and then `Booster$to_predictor()` to get a `Predictor`.

https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/blob/b462d0a40f6978c0a66a47af4522c2ae12532316/R-package/R/lgb.Booster.R#L509-L511

But I would be surprised if many users do that, so for all intents and purposes `Predictor` is private today.

> Is it possible to refactor `.PARAMETER_ALIASES()` in a way it will call cpp function only once for the session

Yes, it is. Briefly, I can think of at least 3 options:

1. Have an environment in the R package's namespace, and have calls to `.PARAMETER_ALIASES()` check that environment for a list like `parameter_aliases`, and only re-populate it if its missing
    - this could also possibly be loaded at the time the package is loaded, using the special `.onLoad()` hook (see ["load hooks" in Writing R extensions](https://cran.r-project.org/doc/manuals/r-release/R-exts.html#Load-hooks))
2. Use the `{memoise}` package ([link](https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/memoise/index.html)), which has functionality similar to `functools.lru_cache()` in Python.

This issue is larger than just this PR, since `.PARAMETER_ALIASES()` is used many times in the R package. I'll write up a separate issue describing this and link it here.

- Suppose we start a ms timer with 10ms interval at this time
- `sMsAlarm` uses `otPlatAlarmMilliGetNow()` so it will set `sMsAlarm <- 11`

```python
"""
Script that bugs cxxtypes.py

import numpy as np

# pythran export _core_decode(float[:, :], float[:], float[:], int[:, :, :], int[:, :], int, int, float)
def _core_decode(arg0, arg1, arg2, arg3, arg4, arg5, arg6, arg7):

    var0 = np.zeros((arg5, 16), dtype=float)
    var1 = np.zeros((arg5, 16, 4), dtype=float)

    for k in range(arg5):
        var2 = [arg2[k], arg1[k]]

        for i in range(16):
            var1[k, i, 0] = np.dot(arg3[i, 0], var2)

            for j in range(1, 4):
                var1[k, i, j] = arg0[k, j - 1] + np.dot(arg3[i, j], var2)

    var3 = 0
    var4 = np.zeros(16, dtype=float)
    var5 = False
    while var3 < arg6 and not var5:
        for k in range(arg5):
            for j in range(16):
                var4[j] = np.max([var0[k, arg4[j, 0]] +
                                  var1[k, arg4[j, 0], 0],
                                  var0[k, arg4[j, 1]] +
                                  var1[k, arg4[j, 1], 1],
                                  var0[k, arg4[j, 2]] +
                                  var1[k, arg4[j, 2], 2],
                                  var0[k, arg4[j, 3]] +
                                  var1[k, arg4[j, 3], 3]])

            var4 = var4 - var4[0]

            if k == arg5 - 1:
                if np.max(np.abs(var4 - var0[0, :])) < arg7:
                    var5 = True
                    break

                var0[0, :] = var4

            else:
                if np.max(np.abs(var4 - var0[k + 1, :])) < arg7:
                    var5 = True
                    break

                var0[k + 1, :] = var4

        var3 += 1

    return var1, var0
```

> ### RE: optional inputs
> > we don't only need to determine which value to use, we also need to determine if the value is pass-by-value or pass-by-reference.
> 
> It sounds like we agree that one execution of a training_info algorithm looks like:
> 
> 1. Evaluate the algorithm graph
> 2. Update the constants _according to update_binding_
> 
> Inference (step 1) has no capability for mutation; only the update step (step 2) does, and the update step is precisely defined by the `update_binding` map. The update binding is not affected by whether you use an optional input or not, or what those inputs are

Yes!

> -- there is no implicit backprop and update, only explicit binding of which constants to update, and explicit calculation of what the new values should be.

What method is used in your mind for passing optional inputs into the inference graph? Pass-by-value? Pass-by-reference? Pass-by-reference may lead to implicit back propagation edge.

Pass-by-value example:
```python
W_init = Tensor([1, 2, 3]) # Global constant.
def inference_graph(X, W=None): # W is optional.
  if W == None:
    W = deepcopy(W_init) # W is NOT a reference to W_init; we pass W in by value.
  Y = X * W
  return Y
X_1 = Tensor([-1, 0, 1])
X_2 = Tensor([2, 4, 6])
Y_1 = inference_graph(X_1)
Y_2 = inference_graph(X_2)
Z = Y_1 + Y_2
```

Pass-by-reference example:

```python
W_init = Tensor([1, 2, 3]) # Global constant.
def inference_graph(X, W=None): # W is optional
  if W == None:
    W = W_init # W is a reference to W_init; we pass W in by reference.
  Y = X * W
  return Y
X_1 = Tensor([-1, 0, 1])
X_2 = Tensor([2, 4, 6])
Y_1 = inference_graph(X_1)
Y_2 = inference_graph(X_2)
Z = Y_1 + Y_2
```

> 
> Here's a somewhat contrived example:
> 
> ```
> graph MyInferenceGraph (%x, optional %w) {
>   default %w = [1]
>   return MatMul(%x, %w)
> }
> graph algorithm (%data, %label) {
>   // We have access to initializers from MyInferenceGraph, namely the default %w
> 
>   // Forward pass with no override of %w
>   %score = GraphCall(%data, "", graph_name="MyInferenceGraph")
> 
>   // Forward pass where we for some reason scale %w by 1.1
>   %s = [1.1]
>   %larger_w = Mul(%w, %s)
>   %score1 = GraphCall(%data, %larger_w, graph_name="MyInferenceGraph")
> 
>   // Ensemble the two forward passes
>   %ensemble_score = Mean(%score, %score1)
> 
>   // Compute delta we want to subtract from %w
>   %d_label = Sub(%label, %ensemble_score)
>    // dw/dlabel at w=%w (not 1.1*%w)
>   %d_w = Gradient(%data, %w, xs=["%data", "%w"], y="%d_label")
>   
>   // Explicitly calculate new value for %w
>   %new_w = Sub(%w, %d_w)
>   return %new_w
> }
> ```

If we choose pass-by-value to pass optional inputs into the first `GraphCall`, the first `GraphCall` has no effect on `%d_w`. In contrast, if we choose pass-by-reference, the following two calls are equivalent

```
%score = GraphCall(%data, "", graph_name="MyInferenceGraph")
```

```
%score = GraphCall(%data, "", graph_name="MyInferenceGraph")
```

Thus, the first `GraphCall` may affect the value of `%d_w`.

It seems to me the first one is more convenient to model authors. The second one is more compiler friendly (because no implicit edges is in computation graph). I think we should do `pass-by-reference` now (yesterday on Gitter, I prefer `pass-by-value`).

> def train(model, x):

>         model = update(model, outputs)
>     return model
> ```
>

If the author wants to loop through a data set, the training graph should contain a loop which loops to the given input. This makes the behavior of these tests reliant on the machine they are run on and things like the directories included in the main config file below are Debian specific. I think in an ideal world, we'd remove all this and make the Apache configuration independent of the machine it is run on like is done in the nginx integration tests, but I think doing so is hard/impossible because Apache binaries normally need things like shared object files to enable TLS.

Since this is not feasible and [configuring the Apache plugin to use an alternate directory is not fully supported from inside the plugin](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/issues/7385) and as a result requires a lot of extra complexity in the tests, I think we should run these tests in Docker where we have a consistent environment and just modify `/etc/apache2` as we wish.

Stepping back even further about how to test the Apache plugin though, I think one of the most complicated and necessary to test parts of the plugin is all the overrides on different OSes. I think it'd be nice if in the long term the Apache integration tests and the "test farm tests" that do this shared as much code as possible. I don't see why there needs to be any difference between the Apache integration tests run on one system and the tests run as part of the future "test farm" tests on the same system.

With all this in mind, I think it may make sense for us to block adding Apache integration tests on rewriting the test farm tests. Then as part of testing PRs or snaps, we could run the Apache "test farm tests" but on a smaller set of systems to speed things up. There are other ways we could go about reusing code between these two types of tests too, but this to me initially seems like it might be the simplest.

The new tolerance of `assert_allclose` ([numpy doc](https://docs.scipy.org/doc/numpy/reference/generated/numpy.testing.assert_almost_equal.html) mentions to use `assert_allclose` over `assert_almost_equal`) is now 1e-10, which means that it is more stricter now when checking for NDArray not being changed. 

A consequence of this as it is used in `assert_mutated` is, it also allows smaller updates, i.e. -5.9604645e-08 (failure case update) to be valid change . 

Thus now we have stricter check on whether any element of NDArray has changed or not (with stricter tolerance) while allowing smaller valid updates (i.e. anything above `rtol` of 1e-10 will be a valid update).

> I think it should be I don't care about 7th(or 8th precision or anything above) precision, it can go arbitrary lower than what I care about.

(**I don't know much about this but**) With the instability of floating point number (rounding errors), I guess it is better to have some tolerance like 1e-10. I don't know if it is good idea to go to Machine Epsilon (lowest possible) as `rtol` for this.

Let me try to shed some light on this... `RelatedMixin` mistakenly shadowed `get_serializer_class` to get related serializer. Shadowing happened as `RelatedMixin` only works when used with a DRF generic view which provides a [get_serializer_class method](https://www.django-rest-framework.org/api-guide/generic-views/#get_serializer_classself) 
For `RelatedMixin` to be able to get parent serializer it then simply used `self.serializer_class` which is not DRF conform. However this only happened when [related urls](https://django-rest-framework-json-api.readthedocs.io/en/stable/usage.html#related-urls) have been configured as otherwise this [if](https://github.com/django-json-api/django-rest-framework-json-api/blob/master/rest_framework_json_api/views.py#L153) is always false and there is no difference than when the method hasn't been overwritten in `RelatedMixin`.

If someone overwrote `get_serializer_class` in its view without using related urls it would just work as `RelatedMixin` did not do anything anyway. If someone tried to overwrite `get_serializer_class` with related urls this user would run into exactly the same problem as @uliSchuster has.

Therefore I don't see that this change affects existing users.

I guess I had no objection in the original PR, but isn't it kind of inefficient to implement the read operation in terms of listing?  The list operation already iterates over all the tensors once in order to determine max step and wall time, but then the read operation doesn't return that metadata at all (it's just discarded) so the computation is wasted, and we have to iterate over the tensors again a second time to get their actual values.

Performance wise it's probably not the end of the world, but it seems suboptimal.  (The same argument we had for the TB.dev backend might suggest also that we don't really need to return max step and wall time all the time; if we made those optional then listing could be made more efficient and the reuse wouldn't result in as much redundant iteration, although it's still a little duplicative even then.)

Let's use `typing_extensions.Self` instead of defining the exact class as the return type of `build` method.

This is benefitial, because when a class is subclassed, if it inherits this `build` method without explicitly overriding it, even though calling it would produce an instance of that subclass, it would be downcasted to the parent class, as defined here and type-checkers would treat it as such. For more info, check [PEP 673](https://peps.python.org/pep-0673/)

Note that `typing_extensions` is not a runtime dependency, which means it should only be imported on typing time. You can achieve this by using `if typing.TYPE_CHECKING`. Along with this, we prefer to use `from future import __annotations__` to avoid having to use strings with these typying time only imports (without this import: `-> "Self"` , with \_\_annotations\_\_: `-> Self`).

The worst case is NlogN is when all elements go into the same bucket. It depends on whether it's worthy of sacrificing extra NlogN - N **memory read** for a reproducible model. 

For most of our inputs, N is less than 10k, log(10k) is 9. Assume each memory random read is 1us, and io read is 1ms.

Assume a sframe can be fully fit into memory, there's no io read from disk. The read will be N * 1us = 100ms.

The worst penalty for memory read is 80k more reads if we sort the bucket. you pay extra 800 ms. Not that bad.

If sframe is very large, more than 2G, which is the cache manager capacity. we may have some read that needs to be load from disk. Let's say only 10% of them will miss the cache and force the load from disk, you pay N*10%*1ms. If N is 10k, then it's about 1000ms.

> - In the otPlatInfraIf APIs, we do have the infra-If index as input, but this is tied to the BR and specifically RoutingManager functionality.

The Infra interface passed to otPlatDnssd APIs doesn't necessary to be always the AIL selected for border routing. The otPlatDnssd APIs can be reused for TREL which can be functioning on another infra interface. For example, RoutingManager works on wlan0 but TREL works on eth0. This is allowed, right?

For border routing, it's better to advertise SRP service on an AIL only when we can provide connectivity between Thread and this AIL. If we don't pass a Infra interface parameter with the otPlatDnssd APIs, the implementations may just advertise the services on all AILs available on this host (OTBR today is doing so) and we certainly want to help avoid this by telling the implementer which exact infra interface he/she should use. Because it caches the class defined by `context.solver` only once. If we then redefine `context.solver` (e.g. with the solver fixture), we still get the cached one, which might not correspond to the current `context.solver` value.

Example:

1. Test A calls `context.plugin_manager.get_solver_backend()`. `context.solver` is `libmamba` (new default in this PR). We get `conda_libmamba_solver.solver.LibmambaSolver`.
2. Test B redefines `context.subdir` (e.g. via `with env_var("CONDA_SOLVER", "classic")`). It invokes this helper, which would call `context.plugin_manager.get_solver_backend()` again, and since it's cached, we still get the libmamba solver, despite expecting `conda.core.solve.Solver`.

I guess this line was added because some tests failed with the new bootstrap tests (because it seems that they were depending on the CLI tests). But imho this doesn't belong here, because the `LocalstackContainer` class does not define any volume mounts for the config dirs on its own.

The `mkdirs` creates the directories depending on where it's executed. If it's executed in Docker, it will create the directories for within the Docker container. If it's executed on the host, it will create the directories for the host system.

The creation of the directories at runtime - within the container - is invoked in `start_infra`:
https://github.com/localstack/localstack/blob/f48dbda6218901e0d41dc0e5ac8cb9c6d67df35f/localstack/services/infra.py#L373

In our case here, since it's executed before the container is started, it will create the _host directories_.
Since these mounts are not configured by the LocalstackContainer class, but by a using component, it is also in the responsibility of this using compontent to create the directories. 
For starting with the CLI, the directories are created in `prepare_docker_start`:
https://github.com/localstack/localstack/blob/71cfcfe651094214300b15ef2e73bb4f1b44a257/localstack/utils/bootstrap.py#L517
This is a utility function used in combination with `bootstrap.configure_container`, which configures the volumes.

TL/DR: Creating the dirs is in the responsibility of the users of this class. Please move this line to the caller(s).

It felt to me that using a function instead of a dict to transform names is more convenient and clear in some ways, but I'm not completely sure which is better. If we use a dict, then we could take one of two approaches. 
One, the dict is required to contain all type names and field names. This would require the user to get all types of a schema, which can be inconvenient.
Two, the dict contains some subset of types and fields, and all other types and fields pass through without change. I feel like this approach could lead to errors, but I'm not completely sure.

In particular, I imagined this being used something like a namespace, by prefixing a string to all names of a schema. In that case, a func seems much more convenient than a dict.
If the user only wants to rename a few conflicting names, they can still define their rename_func to be something like: if input string is a key in this dict, return the value associated with that key, otherwise return the original string.
From a test perspective we yield better coverage if some of these use the default value (not specified), and some use an empty list

Yeah, I was wondering if this was "too much magic": https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi-aws/commit/a77712011c3dc7577a2737b5b3004ee529ea5dc6#r41448321

The motivation for allowing empty bodies was so you wouldn't have to specify names twice (if writing this more verbose form by hand): once in the declaration of the function and again inside the function's body. But I'm definitely open to just emitting the actual implementation in the generated code, where that doesn't matter (and too much "magic" is confusing / hard to debug).

If we did leave the bodies empty, we could theoretically generate faster lookup code in some cases. That's not being done currently, but in the cases where we need to translate the property names a couple times per invocation, we could generate that code ahead-of-time, rather than doing the translations at runtime. But I doubt this would make much of a measurable difference in practice.

This format requires a latter line that says what the link is, like:

```
.. _`@der-gabe`: https://github.com/der-gabe
```

The primary benefit is that the prose isn't littered with hyperlinks and weird line breaks, and you can link to the same word twice. I think that's why I used it, but maybe it is more confusing now and in the future.

Alternatively, you can use inline links, like:
```suggestion
  Thanks `@der-gabe <https://github.com/der-gabe>`_
```

This might be better for the history doc, since releases are rare and "Thanks `@foo <https://github.com/foo>`" will easily fit on a line.

Also, no matter how much I work with RST / Sphinx, I always have to go find documentation on the syntax format. It just doesn't stick in my memory like Markdown. But, it has more features than MD for docs, so it is still worth struggling with.

If I understand correctly, this does have the effect that `_load_fn` and
`_cached_module` leak onto the actual module being imported, and in the
event that the underlying module actually defines those properties they
will not be accessible (because of the `__getattr__`/`__getattribute__`
distinction). This might sound like “who would ever do that”, but it can
happen if you try to lazily load a module that is itself lazily loaded:

```python
>>> from tensorboard import lazy
>>> @lazy.lazy_load("inner")
... def inner():
...   import sys
...   return sys
... 
>>> @lazy.lazy_load("outer")
... def outer():
...   return inner
... 
>>> outer.stdout
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "tensorboard/lazy.py", line 55, in __getattr__
    return getattr(self._cached_module, item)
AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'stdout'
>>> inner.stdout
<open file '<stdout>', mode 'w' at 0x7fa4487f5150>
>>> outer.stdout
<open file '<stdout>', mode 'w' at 0x7fa4487f5150>
>>> 
```

This seems a bit insidious. Can we fix the leaky abstraction? For
instance, we could store `_load_fn` and `_cached_module` in a closure
value instead of on the object itself.

right, because it's not meant to get the parent dependencies, it's meant to get all of the reverse dependencies.  That is, every single dependency that is in the chain of dependencies demanding this specific package.

That is, if you installed `A`, and `A -> B -> C`, the point of `reverse_dependencies` is to tell you about both `B` and `C`, this way you can figure out everything in the way of removing or upgrading a specific package.

The implementation reason for this is that if you want to introduce or update a new package, and that package in turn needs to make a modification to another package already locked in the lockfile, you can't safely do that unless you know about all of the things that are in the dependency chain which rely on the version which has been pinned.  That means you need a way to get that information.

I want to comment on this: why are asserts not allowed? I agree it is completely wrong to use asserts to convey error messages to users. They are useful however for debugging purposes, in order to formally specify invariants that should always be satisfied in any execution, i.e. regardless of user "mistakes". In that sense, they serve the same purpose as unit tests. In fact, running a piece of code and verifying that all asserts pass is a valuable unit test in itself.

Here, I'm just saying that 'm3' should always match since 'st' is a substring of 'prelimFigure'. I could just write it as a comment, but I might as well check it. If this assert fails, it's a programming error of mine, and not a user error, so this should never be reported to the user, but rather, the code should be sufficiently tested to ensure that the assert never fails.

Anyway, this might boil down to coding style, so I'm happy to simply remove this line. Furthermore, Python has the nasty behaviour of enabling asserts by default (i.e. having '__debug__' on by default), when the opposite should be the default, so that the asserts are only verified at testing phase, and this invalidates my argument to some extent.

First off, the race condition here is that a thread can not be started more than once (else an exception is thrown). 

The first check is done outside of the lock as an optimization. If `self.started` is `True` then the thread has already started so there's no need to acquire the lock. Now if `self.started` is `False` we don't know if another thread might also have just read the same condition (since there's no lock). So the lock is acquired and then we check again to achieve the mutual exclusion.

Well, it is a really good question. It was the first thing in my mind: reinjecting a corrected os module in the modules pool of current Python instance. Then it would be used seamlessly by any Certbot module.

There is a lot of problems with that approach eventually.

First, the corrected os module would be used not only by certbot modules, but also by every dependency of Certbot. As you can see, the functionalities added are very specific to Certbot needs. Because in fact, Unix and Windows security models are just too different to have a global and bijective representation in both platforms (otherwise it would be implemented in the standard library). So it means that this model could not be suited for the dependencies, and add unexpected behavior to them. This security model, and so the associated modules, should only be used for the sole Certbot purpose.

Second, the os module is swapped only when the relevant code to manipulate sys.modules is called. Obviously, it should be added to the `__init__.py` of `certbot` package. But also to any other package, like the ones from dns plugins and authenticators, to avoid to break any permission. And ensure this, is the same problem as ensuring that standard `os` is not usedm and should be corrected by a lint process. So lint rules may or may not be used to enforce this policy, but using an injection in sys.modules, do not remove on its own the requirement to check the code.

Finally, even if implicit approach fit quite well in Python, I think there is a limit, related to good or bad design decisions. Making implicit modifications of one of the most used standard library feels wrong to me. I largely prefer to be explicit about using a different `os` module, and make it clear from the code.

1. Users may want to use an epoch based repodata if the repodata with the lastest releases does not resolve.  One could imagine a system with multiple layers of fall backs. Each attempt to solve the environment with a particular repodata will have a time cost.  A preferred sparse repodata followed by a single fallback seems like a good initial step before implementing a fully layered approach.
2. Each solve will take time and if a solution is not found in the preferred repodata file that is time wasted.  The preferred repodata file default to a version with just the latest version of all packages which should be small and quick to process and check for a solution. The conda solver prefers these version when installing unbound specification so I expect that solutions will be found regularly via this method.  Basically, simple solves which use the latest version should be very quick (<5 seconds).  More complex solves which require the fallback repodata and second or two overhead to check the "easy" solution should not be a significant overhead.

The setup.py and requirements.txt do different things. The setup.py is package metadata used in the installation of the package: it contains hard runtime dependencies, and optional runtime dependencies under "extras", with loose-as-possible version constraints (if it's a library or has to coexist with other python packages). The requirements.txt is for developers and testers of the package: it contains all possible runtime dependencies, ideally with fixed versions to test against, and all test, doc, format, and build dependencies.

Without a requirements.txt, someone interested in contributing has to dig through the setup.py (and hope the extras are all well-described), all of the CI config, the Makefile, and potentially all of the test files to find what they actually need to install to make sure it works. With a requirements.txt, it's `git clone...; pip install -r requirements.txt` and they're away.

OpenType tags have [different requirements](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/typography/opentype/spec/otff#data-types) than what is stated here:
> A Tag value is a uint8 array. Each byte within the array must have a value in the range 0x20 to 0x7E. This corresponds to the range of values of Unicode Basic Latin characters in UTF-8 encoding, which is the same as the printable ASCII characters. As a result, a Tag value can be re-interpreted as a four-character sequence, which is conventionally how they are referred to. Formally, however, the value is a byte array. When re-interpreted as characters, the Tag value is case sensitive. It must have one to four non-space characters, padded with trailing spaces (byte value 0x20). A space character cannot be followed by a non-space character.

>Also, aren't indices supposed to be unique? As in - there must be more than one record with the same to and subject right?

No, the default index you get when using this in Postgres (btree) performs worse as the cardinality of the data shrinks. Cardinality means "number of value possibilities" in this sense. So there is no point adding an index to say a boolean field or a field with three choice values - because there are only two or three options retrospectively and in those scenarios it is most likely better for the query planner to pick a full table scan rather than use an index which isn't going to be much help.

https://distributedsystemsauthority.com/index-efficiency-and-maintenance-postgresql-12-high-performance-guide-part-5-12/#:~:text=Low%20cardinality%20means%20a%20lot,example%2C%20the%20primary%20key%20column.

NaN is not the same thing as None (which Pandas is finding, and that's why they're trying to deprecate all the methods that looked for NaN in favor of option-types). However, in

```python
>>> narr =np.array([np.nan, np.nan, 1, -1, 30])
>>> np.sort(narr)
array([-1.,  1., 30., nan, nan])
```

it looks like it's being sorted the same way. If NaN is insensitive to the choice of `ascending=True` vs `ascending=False`, that's to be expected because all comparisons with NaN result in false:

```python
>>> a = np.nan
>>> b = np.nan
```

would return false from `a < b` and also from `b < a`. So if ascending puts Nones at one end of the list and descending puts Nones at the other, but the same isn't true for NaN, I'd say that's okay. NaN is not None.

For the other case,

The former. The code generated by our RPC parser doesn't work for our use-case. Basically, the problem is that for GET requests with nested data dicts , like `{"pagination" : { "max_results": 1 }}` the request the CLI produces is `GET /pipelines?pagination=max_results` which is obviously wrong. 

My fix turns `{"pagination" : { "max_results": 1 }}` into `{"pagination.max_results": 1}, which produces `/pipelines?pagination.max_results=1`, which the server can parse and handle correctly. What we need to do in code generator is this, essentially: recursively flatten all nested dicts for GET requests, while path-compressing the keys.

I have investigated this issue before I decided to remove "with" statement. "TTCollection" class does not provide the "close" method. Therefore, adding `font.close()` is impracticable. However, due to garbage collector of Python, the font object will be automatically closed and deleted, but heavily relying on garbage collector is a bad practice. Of course, there are at least two workarounds:
1. Re-implement the close method:
```
if font.reader is not None:
   font.reader.close()
```
2. Open font file by built-in open:
```
with open(font_path, 'rb') as f:    
    try:
        font = TTFont(f)
    except TTLibError:
        font = TTCollection(f)

    temp_path = get_temp_file_path()
    font.saveXML(temp_path, tables=tables)
    return temp_path
```
Nevertheless, both methods, I think, will add  maintenance burden if the "TTCollection" class implement close method in some day.

Passing a pointer in and of itself is memory-allocation-neutral. Using a raw pointer makes it more clear that ownership of the memory is not changing; using a `unique_ptr` leaves open the possibility that the function can delete the memory.

`NamedTemporaryFile` `delete` defaults to `True`, and this algorithm relies on an open file handle for the duration of the runtime invocation, and breaks if the `certifi` module is reloaded, as other modules will have the old `where()` result.

> The concern is roll-out order?

No. The feature support checking will take care of this. SDKs will only send output values if the monitor says it supports them, and then engine will only pass output values to a provider if it says it accepts them. And even when accepting output values, if the code that's accepting the output values doesn't have `KeepOutputValues` specified when unmarshaling, it will get back the same values as previously.

I'm mainly concerned that output values will somehow show up in places that can't fully accommodate them (yet), both (1) within an SDK and (2) within the engine. Perhaps I'm being overly paranoid, but given the short timeframe for rolling this out, and being involved with a similar feature (resource references) that was enabled more broadly and had a bit of a bug tail, I'm inclined to really scope down where the feature is enabled as much as possible initially.

I'm less concerned about (1), because I've done a scrub of the places where we marshal and keep output values in the SDKs. For example, there are places in the SDK where we roundtrip inputs (i.e. marshal and then unmarshal), e.g. for mocks-based testing. In that case, since the mock monitor doesn't support the feature, it should all just work as expected.

Slightly more concerned about (2). When unmarshaling the RPC values, if `KeepOutputValues` isn't true, we should get the exact same behavior as before (any output values will be unmarshaled in the old way) so we should be covered. However, if somehow output values _did_ show up, such as enabling it for _all_ `RegisterResource` calls, I'm concerned the output values might somehow mess up areas that haven't been looked at closely, such as diffs, where previously `resource.Output` was treated as unknown.

Again, these concerns are theoretical because I don't think we actually have any problems here, I'm just aiming to reduce risk.

The issue is that `CGenerator` does not print quals for `Decl`. I.e. the following AST is generated:

```
auto const _Atomic(int *) a;
FileAST(ext=[Decl(name='a',
                  quals=['const'
                        ],
                  storage=['auto'
                          ],
                  funcspec=[
                           ],
                  type=PtrDecl(quals=['_Atomic'
                                     ],
                               type=TypeDecl(declname='a',
                                             quals=[
                                                   ],
                                             type=IdentifierType(names=['int'
                                                                       ]
                                                                 )
                                             )
                               ),
                  init=None,
                  bitsize=None
                  )
            ]
        )
```
and then the generator turns it into:
```
auto int * _Atomic a;

FileAST(ext=[Decl(name='a',
                  quals=[
                        ],
                  storage=['auto'
                          ],
                  funcspec=[
                           ],
                  type=PtrDecl(quals=['_Atomic'
                                     ],
                               type=TypeDecl(declname='a',
                                             quals=[
                                                   ],
                                             type=IdentifierType(names=['int'
                                                                       ]
                                                                 )
                                             )
                               ),
                  init=None,
                  bitsize=None
                  )
            ]
        )
```

An obvious fix is to add `quals` printing in `def _generate_decl(self, n):`.

```diff
diff --git a/pycparser/c_generator.py b/pycparser/c_generator.py
index ded8c65..5a977e5 100644
--- a/pycparser/c_generator.py
+++ b/pycparser/c_generator.py
@@ -418,6 +418,7 @@ class CGenerator(object):
         s = ''
         if n.funcspec: s = ' '.join(n.funcspec) + ' '
         if n.storage: s += ' '.join(n.storage) + ' '
+        if n.quals: s += ' '.join(n.quals) + ' '
         s += self._generate_type(n.type)
         return s
 ```

But that route is a bit complicated as it leads to duplicating quals, e.g. `_Atomic int x;` gives `_Atomic _Atomic int x;` now. This feels wrong in the source, because I see the `_Atomic` qualifier being present twice in the AST:

```
FileAST(ext=[Decl(name='x',
                  quals=['_Atomic'
                        ],
                  storage=[
                          ],
                  funcspec=[
                           ],
                  type=TypeDecl(declname='x',
                                quals=['_Atomic'
                                      ],
                                type=IdentifierType(names=['int'
                                                          ]
                                                    )
                                ),
                  init=None,
                  bitsize=None
                  )
            ]
        )
```

Was the original intention to duplicate quals in both `Decl` and `TypeDecl` but use Decl for user interaction only? This feels wrong, as I cannot put `const` in the first example to e.g. `PtrDecl` `quals`, as it would then refer to the inner type.

To make it clearer, I list several possible situations:

1. Users call add_loss inside functions decorated by CollectLoss, add_loss appends losses into _losscache, _losscache would then get cleared in CollectLoss, len(_losscache) becomes 0 when __call__ is invoked.

2. Users call add_loss without using CollectLoss, add_loss appends losses into _losscache, _losscache  still contains value when entering  __call__, in this case, a exception will be raised.

3. Users use CollectLoss without calling add_loss, self._losses = out[1] = []

4. Users use StochasticBlock without calling CollectLoss or add_loss, len(out) == 1, out[1] will not be accessed.

Yes. So the issue is that for asyncio actor, we have two sets of threads to run actor methods: boost fiber threads and python asyncio threads. When a actor method call task comes in, a boost detached fiber will post it to python asyncio thread and wait for it to finish so the fiber can sends the response back to the caller. During shutdown, we simply stop running python coroutines instead of waiting for them to finish (which is impossible for infinite loop actor method), as a result, waiting boost fiber never receives the notification that the corresponding python coroutine finishes so they won't terminate which prevents the fiber thread from being joined.

The `self._next_pos` is not updated when data is read and so it will be before `self._pos` after a `read`. That's why `self._pos` needs to be returned in `tell` and used in `seek` as `self._next_pos` is not up-to-date.

My aim was to not re-write the code as it's probably a matter of style how to implement it. That means that `self._next_pos` is only a valid file position after a `seek` where it is guaranteed to be > `self._pos`. Otherwise `self._pos` should be used as the file position.

```
for line in process.stdout:
    _extract_state(target, output, status)
process.wait()

```
A lot of the unofficial examples online seem to use `readline` to read from stdout, but I see no reason this needs to be done.

I also think an empty line will never be empty before `EOF` is reached because it will always at least contain a newline character and we'll block on one being written to the stream.

https://docs.python.org/3/library/subprocess.html#subprocess.Popen.returncode describes when `returncode` is set. Using `wait` should be redundant with the `for `loop (maybe unless `snapcraft` does something like closing its stdout), but making sure the process has finished doesn't seem like a bad idea to me. We could also just have `return process.wait()` as the final line.

This would be good to test out in the `studies` directory because it's the logic of caching that you're concerned about, and it can be replicated in Python without needing much preexisting code. You can just use a dict for a cache and write up a simple VirtualArray class, then try it out in a variety of situations to see if it's making sense.

Unfortunately, I don't have a good starting implementation to use as a guide: https://awkward-array.readthedocs.io/en/latest/ak.layout.VirtualArray.html But maybe you can fill in the missing methods with the implementation before `ptr_lib_`, see how it works, and then add `ptr_lib_` to see if your algorithm is still sensible.

"Sensible" means that you should avoid filling the cache with two copies of the same array, only the one on the desired device, but you can't expect users to generate them in the right place, and they might use the same cache for CPU and GPU arrays, so it should deal with that in a way that copies as little as possible. You don't need anything realistic for the non-VirtualArray types; just a dummy class tagged with a `ptr_lib_`.

```
For operators that allow a UDF/SDF as input, the output types can
be specified by the developer (i.e., “Specified” in Table 1), or left
unspecified. Suppose a developer wants to perform a map with a
SDF that returns TRUE for non-NULL values, and FALSE otherwise.
Since all columns in the output are known to be boolean, this information can be provided by the developer implementing the SDF
to the map upfront to avoid costly type inference. Alternatively,
when the types of the output dataframe after a map is not known, it
ends up being UNSPECIFIED for every column.
```

I think the precomputing dtypes should  happend at the qc.

How do you import `ocrd.Workspace` and `ocrd_network.models.Workspace` in the same context without having to use a placeholder for either of the two, e.g.:

```python
from ocrd.workspace import Workspace
from ocrd_network.models.database import Workspace as SOME_PLACEHOLDER
from ocrd_network.models.workspace import Workspace as SOME_OTHER_PLACEHOLDER
```

And any time these are needed in the same context, different placeholders will be introduced. Not to mention all the circular import problems I had to deal with in the reference WebAPI implementation in the past.

vs.

```python
from ocrd.workspace import Workspace
from ocrd_network.models.database import DBWorkspace
from ocrd_network.models.workspace import PYWorkspace
```

Where the placeholders are no longer needed.

Moreover, why should a user of library care know the exact path of imported things? As a user, I would rather do:
```python
from ocrd_network import DBWorkspace, PYWorkspace
```

than 

```python
from ocrd_network.models.database import Workspace as SOME_PLACEHOLDER
from ocrd_network.models.workspace import Workspace as SOME_OTHER_PLACEHOLDER
```

The second one is asking for troubles when the paths no longer match for whatever reason.

Let's say we have 4 arrays of size 1, 2, 4, 1

Before: `max_size = 4` and `max_index = 2` are computed beforehand and when `i = 1` we get the message:
> Cannot expand array of size 2 to size 4

After: `max_size` and `max_index` are computed in a loop, when `i = 2` we have `max_size = 2`, `max_index = 1` and `array.size = row[i].size = 4` the conditions `array.size > max.size` and `max_size > 1` are true and the message is:
> Cannot expand array of size 2 to size 4

And then `max_size` becomes 4 and `max_index` becomes 2

I do believe what you're seeking is a feature that I'd like to consider separately - that of verifying that a Distribution and its transitive dependencies are satisfied.

This feature is meant primarily to determine which additional dependencies are required for a given entry point and to report any that aren't already satisfied, leaving it to another function to handle what to do about that condition (install the dependency, raise an error, log a warning, ...).

I'd like to leave it as an additional exercise/feature to perform checks.

But now that I think about it, perhaps your right and this feature adds little value. After all, whatever feature checks for the required dependencies of an entry point may still want to check that the dependencies are met for the distribution (with relevant extras).

Something like `iter_unmet_dependencies(dist, extras=[])`, which could be called with `ep.dist, ep.extras` to validate an entry point.

At this point, I'm convinced the proposed feature and the 'unmet dependencies' feature is undesirable for this package (mainly because 'packaging' is unavailable). I'll work on creating a separate library that provides this functionality.

> Regarding the asynchronously running: This was the only way for me to make it work. I think for your suggestion to work the transport (or the channel, I don't know) has to be kept so it can be used later to shut it down.

Yes.

> I currently prefer to start the workers and then let them run independently without an open connection or something like that. I think/thought (without a really good reason maybe) it is better to not keep a connection. Then other things would have to be worried about, keeping it going, handle failures etc.
> But I have not much experience regarding this. What are the benefits of using the channel object and keeping it connected?

Note: paramiko.transport by default does not send keep-alive messages, but can be [set up](https://docs.paramiko.org/en/stable/api/transport.html#paramiko.transport.Transport.set_keepalive) to do so.

IMO the benefit of keeping the session open would be that you don't have to worry about detaching and PID tracking in the first place. (The fact that paramiko does not provide access to the PID itself is a good argument against such tricks.)

But as I said in the other comment, perhaps this has to be seen in practice first, so let's try with your current implementation. 

(That being said, I do think there are better ways to attain the PID value. For example, you could do something like `echo $! > $processor.pid`, with `$processor` defined and passed by the client a priori, and then in `kill_processing_worker` do `kill $(cat $processor.pid)`. Or instead of `paramiko` use `asyncssh`, which directly provides a function [Connection.create_subprocess](https://asyncssh.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api.html#asyncssh.SSHClientConnection.create_subprocess) that lets you access its [get_pid](https://asyncssh.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api.html#asyncssh.SSHSubprocessTransport.get_pid). Or have the Processing Worker write its PID into the database under a new worker model. )

Your implementation accepts desired vFolder status as an enum, which is also used to represent vFolder current status. The problem is that desired vFolder status and current vFolder status slightly differs its meaning. "current" vFolder status should represent as-is, e.g. Copying something, Cloning to new vFolder, being deleted, and so on. In contrast to previous term, "desired" condition should only distinguish between "ready to read" (superset of all created vFolders) or "ready to alter" (this should mean all vFolders which are only in "READY" status). Using same enum to represent two different categories can be confused to other readers reading this code. How about creating new Enum to represent desired vFolder condition? This value should be determined by current vFolder status (`vfolder.status` column), so it won't be added to database scheme.

class VFolderAccessStatus(str, enum.Enum):

`any()` is used to iterate over the `map()` because `map()` gives us a `map object` and won't actually iterate over `traces_to_process` until we iterate over the `map object`.

It is internal, that's correct. The logic is:
1. On first init (which happens in the process the user started), we read the file and wrap the contents in the internal `AccelerateConfigWrapper`
2. The Trainer class is initialized again inside the Trainable. We read the `accelerate_config` argument and see that it is wrapped, in which case we do not try to load the file from disk again and instead use the string as raw contents.

In other words, this is necessary to deal with the issue of the Trainer being initialized twice (once by the user, the second time in the trainable).

For quite some time there has been more than two image tags – one larger than the other.
I believe it changed to be like this over a year ago now.

I've written up some code to deal with the possibility of different numbers of images.
The code will find the largest image.

I haven't tested the code yet in Calibre, just tested in the Terminal.

```py
if get_cover:
     cover_elements = soup.find('div', {'class': '_4'}).find_all('img')
     image_sources = list(map(lambda x: x['src'], cover_elements))
     largest_image_size = max(list(map(lambda x: re.search(r'\/([\d]{3})\/([\d]{3})\.([a-z]{3})', x).group(1), image_sources)))
     for source in image_sources:
          if re.search(r'\/{0}\/{0}\.'.format(largest_image_size), source):
               cover_url = 'https:' + source
     self.setCoverImage(url, cover_URL)
```

This is interesting. I think that the lifting behavior is wrong in general here: essentially, it's lifting based on the assumption that all parameter types are prompt, so any eventual inputs must transform eventual outputs. Instead, the lifting should be conditional on the argument type not being assignable to the parameter type. In other words, a call that passes an `Output<T>` arg to an `Output<T>` parameter does not require lifting, but a call that passes an `Output<T>` arg to a `T` parameter does.

> No, I take that back - why would that trigger the warning then? A constant expression combined with a non-constant one triggers the warning?

I think what happened: sometime in the 1990s or even 80s someone implemented C4127 for C. It made total sense: the warning would tell you that you could simply remove the (sub)expression (and maybe an entire if or else branch with it). Then templates were added to the language. But nearly a quarter century later, Microsoft still hasn't updated the diagnostic to take that into account, therefore in modern template heavy code most of these warnings are false positives, irritating noise.

I don't think it's useful to convolute constexpr modernization into dealing with the antiquated warning, beyond the coincidence-to-some-degree that a constexpr function can be used as a trick to silence the warning.

```Python
import numpy as np

def p1(delta, order=3):
    return delta[:, np.newaxis] ** np.arange(order + 1)

def p2(delta, order=3):
    return np.stack([delta[:, np.newaxis] ** o for o in np.arange(order + 1)], axis=-1)

for n in [10, 100, 256, 1000, 10000, 1000000]:
    delta = np.random.randn(n)
    print(f"\nSize {n}:")
    %timeit p1(delta)
    %timeit p2(delta)
```

I don't recommend setting the link-time flags `-s TOTAL_MEMORY=128MB -s EXIT_RUNTIME=1 -s PROXY_TO_PTHREAD=1` here. Client apps that consume oneTBB may prefer different settings. Only `-pthread` is truly needed here. The other flags should probably be set where the executable targets are defined. For example, the `tbb_add_test()` function in `test/CMakeLists.txt` could apply these settings to each executable test using CMake's `target_link_options()` function.

Also, instead of guessing how much total memory is required, you may wish to use `-s ALLOW_MEMORY_GROWTH=1`. In that case, you can suppress the compiler warning about using threads and growable memory with the `-Wno-pthreads-mem-growth` flag.
